chunk_summary: Lactational mastitis management emphasizes continued breastfeeding, effective milk removal, and appropriate use of antibiotics when necessary, while avoiding practices that may worsen inflammation.
learning_objectives: Understand the spectrum of lactational mastitis and its clinical presentation.
Learn effective management strategies, including continued breastfeeding and milk removal techniques.
Identify indications for antibiotic therapy and appropriate empiric coverage.
Recognize practices to avoid, such as vigorous breast massage or unnecessary pumping.
key_facts: Lactational mastitis ranges from inflammatory milk stasis to bacterial infection.
Symptoms include localized breast pain, erythema, swelling, fever, and myalgias.
Breastfeeding should continue to improve symptoms and prevent milk stasis.
Antibiotics are indicated if systemic symptoms persist or worsen after 24–48 hours of conservative management.
Empiric antibiotic therapy should cover MSSA, with MRSA coverage considered based on local epidemiology.
Avoid vigorous massage and unnecessary pumping to prevent worsening inflammation.
reference_quote: "Breastfeeding should usually continue, because emptying the breast improves symptoms and abrupt cessation increases stasis." (ABM Clinical Protocol #36)
document_key_topics: Avoidance of harmful practices in mastitis management
